title: Traveling with Kyle - Costa Rica Part 2/2, Panama, Equador Part 1/2

transcript.text: Okay, so yes, I'm in Costa Rica, on the western side of the surf place, I surfed for a couple days.
And I, uh, it was kind of a touristy place, although, um, and then I went, and it was pretty too,
And then I went and I saw, there was a sign, like, it was like a massage place or something.
So I go in there and I'm like, hey, cool, let's do a trade.
And so it was like a guy and a woman, and so I gave the woman a Thai massage,
And the guy wanted to give me some kind of energy massage or something.
And then he wanted to hang out with me and watch soccer games with me and stuff over at my bungalow, so he did that.
And then, um, I rode my bike in the next day into, um, down into, uh, Panama, crossed over into Panama,
And rode down about halfway down Panama towards, uh, Panama City.
And I stayed, one place I stayed, the hotel, I went out one night and I did a couple of karaoke songs, kind of fun.
And, but then I got kind of bored and I decided to just take the bus into Panama City.
And I hung out in Panama City a couple days, which is a very fascinating place.
I got a hotel in Smack Dab in the middle of Panama City, their plaza.
In Latin America, every city has a plaza.
Um, and so that plaza signifies the center of the, uh, historical city.
Actually, this, this wasn't officially the historical city of Panama.
I guess the historical one was like maybe 10 miles south or something like that,
But the one of Panama City, historical park.
And I got in this really old, big, big room in this really old hotel.
Um, really nice room, really nice hotel, but it was kind of dilapidated.
It dilapidated right looking over on the second floor, looking over the, the, uh, plaza.
And it was really cheap.
I guess they didn't have the reagentification.
And a lot of these cities in Latin America, like, they get all, like, snooty or whatever.
And they, uh, decide that it's cool to, like, live in the modern part of town or whatever.
And so then the beautiful historical part just has no value.
And so it was cool.
I felt like I was living it up.
Anyway, I did the, I biked all over Panama.
I rode to the top of, like, a hill there in Panama City and, uh, had a cool view overlooking the city.
And they rode my bike out to the, um, the, uh, what's it called?
The canal.
And did a canal tour and actually saw a boat, uh, go through their passage.
Because, I guess, um, maybe because of the, uh, the way that the tides work.
Um, one, like, the Atlantic Ocean is, like, 20 feet, 30 feet higher than the Pacific Ocean.
So they actually had to, in order so the boats wouldn't get, you know, smashed up against the side of the canal from the current.
They had to actually regulate it with these, uh, things.
So the boat would go into a, into their thing and then get totally blocked off on both sides.
And then it would get pumped up or pumped down 30 feet to the new level.
And then, and then, you know, the gates would open and it would go off.
So it was kind of interesting.
And so I saw, like, a boat do that.
And there's, like, even a, you know, lookout platform to watch the boat do that.
And, um, yeah.
So I hung out and checked out Panama Sea.
Panama Sea is very interesting because it's very, very cosmopolitan.
I don't know.
Maybe because it's, um, you know, all those worldly boats pass through and people decide to get off the boat and live there or something.
But I saw all kinds of people, like, on the pedestrian mall and walking around from all over the world, you know, and dressed in their, like, um, native garb.
I felt like I was in, like, a spaceship with, like, looking at all the different aliens, like, in Star Wars or something, you know, and they're all dressed in their own garb.
It's, like, Indians in Indian clothing and, you know, Native American-looking people and black people and white people.
And I was like, whoa, Panama, whoa, that's cool.
So, um, anyway, uh, but near, pretty close to where my hotel was, I saw, like, uh, I rode my bike past, like, a slummy place, which looked really poor.
And, um, yeah.
So, uh, and there were some, you know, historical buildings and stuff I walked and looked at.
But, oh, and I even rode my bike out to the pier.
There was, like, a pier and lots, and there were lots of people running and biking.
Um, so, it was, I think, you know, more upscale than, like, Managla.
Um, and, uh, yeah.
So, then I decided that it was time for me, I, well, I decided, ah, 422.
Um, I, um, I, uh, so, yeah, originally I was gonna take a plane into, uh, Colombia, but those guys, like, freaked out.
So, I thought maybe that was, like, an omen, maybe I'd be, like, all right, we'll be Colombia some other time.
So, I decided to buy a plane ticket from Panama to Quito, Ecuador, baby.
So, I, you know, and so then I went to ride my bicycle, um, out to the airport.
And, uh, and I was, like, where is the airport?
And so, I'm riding down and I see this guy, this bicycle, this Indian guy on a road bike dressed up in spandex like he's a bike racer.
And so, I pull up next to him and I go, hey, you know, what's up?
Where's the, uh, airport?
And he's, like, oh, yeah, go this way, go that way, and the next way.
So, I go, okay, cool, thanks.
And then I go and I get there to the airport and then, uh, you have, I had to pay 20 extra bucks to get my bike wrapped up in saran wrap.
I'd never seen that happen before.
So, I did that.
It was kind of weird.
And, uh, threw it on the plane.
And I guess there was a problem, though.
Um, Quito is engulfed in clouds or something and it would have been, like, dangerous to land the plane.
So, we had to end up landing the plane in, uh, Gayaquil, uh, which is the, uh, I guess it's officially the capital.
Or, no, no, Quito is the capital, but it's actually, like, the largest city in Ecuador because it's right there on the, uh, the coast of, uh, Pacific.
And, uh, so they put us up in, like, a five-star hotel, dude.
It was awesome.
I loved it.
I mean, most of the people there, they were, like, some, you know, bridge designers and older people.
And they were, like, goddammit, we don't know, we don't want to do this.
But I was thinking, cool, man, cha-ching.
I get to stay in a five-star restaurant and watch movies and stuff.
And there was this other, like, young traveler kid who was like, yeah, this is awesome.
And we went down to the gym.
We were lifting weights there.
And then we had, like, nice breakfast the next morning.
And anyways, I'm having breakfast and I look over and there's this guy.
It was that Indian bike racer guy.
And he was my pilot.
Pretty crazy.
So, anyways, um, then we get back in a plane.
We fly back into Ecuador.
And it was, uh, Quito, it was very, uh, it was exciting to land in Quito.
Because, you know, I was going into South America.
And, uh, you know, I learned Spanish.
And it was, like, you know, my Incans.
And it was just, poof, very, very exciting for me.
And I landed and rode my bike into a hotel.
I can't remember what it was called.
Maybe One World Hotel or something.
Like, I had a gay German or European gay owner or whatever.
And lots of traveler kids, man.
And it was just, like, ultimate party hotel, dude.
Like, tons of travelers.
It was just, like, central travel area.
The guy, actually, who ran the hotel would serve us alcohol.
I think they were called Cuba Libres, rum and Cokes.
And we'd all get drunk and go out to the discos together.
And I ended up staying there three weeks.
And it was, like, when we'd go out to the discos and hang out, it was, like, packs of, like, 20 people.
And it was fun.
And there were some really cool people, man, I hung out with there.
And even the guy who owned the place even had us, like, have, like, games, like, drinking games and other kinds of, like, where he'd show us photos.
And we'd have to guess what part of the world that was in and stuff.
It was fun.
And I, at one point, one Saturday, I tried to ride my bike down to do the bungee jump.
It was, like, they claimed it was the third biggest bungee jump in the world and the biggest in South America.
But I couldn't find it, so I had to wait the next week.
And I did it the next week.
It was, like, 90-meter bungee jump or something.
And maybe, like, 30 of free fall or something.
But anyway, it was crazy.
It was off a bridge.
It was, you go down northeast of the city and down the hill and then go to the ravine.
So I remember, like, only this time I took the bus.
I didn't want to, like, risk getting lost.
So I, anyways, when the gringos go there to bungee jump, I guess if girls do it naked, they can do it for free.
And I guess they haven't even had it like that for guys.
But, you know, guys did it naked so they could do it for free.
So if you're a guy, you just have to pay 50 bucks.
So I get on the side, and there's, like, 30 or 50 Indians.
I guess that's what the Indians do or the natives do on their Saturdays as they watch the green coves do the bungee jumping.
So I go there, and I get there just in time on the side of the ravine to watch this girl standing there.
And I look, and then I see her standing there five or six seconds, and then she does it.
And I was like, holy.
And I'm glad I got to see her do it because, you know, it kind of gave me courage to do it.
But so then I go, and they latch me up, you know, to my feet.
And then I had a backup on my chest.
And they were really good.
They, you know, explained to me exactly, you know, that they were safe and everything.
And I go out on the plank, dude, and I look down the future ravine, you know.
And they do the countdown, and then I waited, like, one second.
And then I jumped.
And, you know, the first couple of seconds, it wasn't really that scary.
But then you start to pick up speed.
And then you can, like, feel the air zoom by you at, like, 100 miles an hour.
And they're, like, roaringly loud.
And you can see the ocean, the river coming at you.
And it's just, it literally tore the screams out of me.
It was the most terrifying experience I have ever had in my life.
I mean, just totally intense.
Like, I mean, you can't even describe it.
I mean, it's not the kind of thing that'll get less scary the next time you do it, you know.
And then, so, yeah, the thing totally extended.
And then I got flung back up almost as high as when I originally started.
And then I had to fall again, you know.
And I'm totally helpless.
There's nothing I can do.
And I screamed again as loud as I could as I came down.
And I bounced, like, 10 times.
And then I remember hanging there after it was all over going,
Oh, my God.
Oh, my God.
Oh, my God.
Oh, my God.
Oh, my God.
And looking down at the river, which wasn't so far below me.
And then they pulled me up.
And I was like, holy shit.
Everybody up there, like, oh, my God.
Oh, my God.
Yeah.
So then I went back up there.
And, oops, my 10 minutes is up.
So I guess this is going to have to continue.
Mm-hmm.
I think this is going to have to go.
Yeah.
So I think this is going to have to go.
I'm going to have to go.
